question: Explain the Open/Closed Principle with a real example.
shortAnswer: Open for extension, closed for modification. In SGN, adding a new notification channel (WhatsApp) required only a new class implementing NotificationSender — zero changes to existing code. The if/else version required modifying the core notification method every time.
detailedAnswer:
Open/Closed Principle: software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.

In practice, this means you can add new behavior without changing existing, tested code. The mechanism is polymorphism — interfaces, abstract classes, strategy pattern.

Real example from SGN:
Before: NotificationService had a switch/case with EMAIL, SMS, PUSH. Adding WhatsApp meant modifying a tested method.
After: NotificationSender interface with implementations. Adding WhatsApp = new class + CDI registration. Zero modifications to existing code.

When NOT to apply:
- Simple code with 2-3 cases that rarely change
- When abstraction adds complexity without real extension points
- YAGNI — do not preemptively abstract for hypothetical extensions
keyPoints: Open for extension = new behavior via new code
Closed for modification = existing code untouched
Real benefit: tested code stays tested
redFlags: Only knowing the definition, no real example
Cannot name when it is NOT appropriate

followUps.question: When would applying OCP be over-engineering?
followUps.hint: Simple switch with 2 stable cases. Abstraction cost > benefit.

followUps.question: How does OCP relate to the Strategy pattern?
followUps.hint: Strategy is the most common mechanism to achieve OCP.

realExperience.title: SGN notification channels
realExperience.description: Refactored notification from switch/case to Strategy pattern. Adding WhatsApp required only a new implementation class — zero changes to existing notification logic.

question: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle and why does it matter?
shortAnswer: Any subtype must be usable wherever the parent type is expected without breaking correctness. It matters because violations create bugs that only appear at runtime — the compiler cannot catch them. Classic violation: Square extending Rectangle breaks setWidth/setHeight expectations.
detailedAnswer:
LSP says: if S is a subtype of T, objects of type T can be replaced with objects of type S without altering the correctness of the program.

Why it matters: when you violate LSP, polymorphism becomes dangerous. Code that works with the parent type breaks silently with certain subtypes.

Classic violations:
- Square extends Rectangle (setting width changes height — breaks caller expectations)
- ReadOnlyList extends List (add() throws UnsupportedOperationException)
- Any override that throws where parent does not, or ignores parent contract

In practice, LSP means:
- Subtypes must honor the contract (preconditions, postconditions, invariants)
- Throwing unexpected exceptions = violation
- Silently ignoring operations = violation
- Strengthening preconditions = violation (accepting less than parent)
- Weakening postconditions = violation (promising less than parent)
keyPoints: Violations create runtime bugs invisible to compiler
redFlags: Cannot explain beyond the textbook definition
No practical example of a violation

followUps.question: Give a real LSP violation you have seen or fixed.
followUps.hint: Think about exceptions thrown in overrides, or methods that silently do nothing.

followUps.question: How does LSP relate to composition over inheritance?
followUps.hint: LSP violations are a signal that inheritance is wrong — use composition instead.

question: How do SOLID principles work together in a real system?
shortAnswer: They are not independent rules — they reinforce each other. SRP creates focused classes. OCP makes them extensible via interfaces. LSP ensures subtypes are safe. ISP keeps interfaces small. DIP wires everything through abstractions. Together, they produce code that is testable, extensible, and maintainable.
detailedAnswer:
SOLID principles are a cohesive set, not a checklist:

1. SRP creates small, focused classes → easier to understand and test
2. OCP says extend via new classes, not modification → requires interfaces (ISP) and polymorphism (LSP)
3. LSP ensures substitutability → makes OCP safe (new implementations do not break callers)
4. ISP keeps interfaces small → clients depend only on what they use → easier DIP
5. DIP inverts dependencies toward abstractions → enables all of the above

In SGN, the report module demonstrates all five:
- SRP: DataSource, Formatter, Exporter are separate concerns
- OCP: new formats added without modifying existing code
- LSP: every Exporter is safely substitutable
- ISP: Exporter interface has only export() — not also format()
- DIP: ReportGenerator depends on interfaces, not concrete classes
keyPoints: DIP is the glue that enables the rest
Over-applying any principle is also harmful
redFlags: Cannot give a concrete example connecting 2+ principles

followUps.question: When have you intentionally violated a SOLID principle?
followUps.hint: Shows pragmatism — sometimes a simple solution is better than a "clean" one.

followUps.question: Which principle is most often violated in legacy code?
followUps.hint: Usually SRP (god classes) or DIP (concrete dependencies everywhere).

realExperience.title: SGN report module applying all 5 principles
realExperience.description: DataSource/Formatter/Exporter separation demonstrates SRP, OCP (new formats without modification), LSP (all exporters substitutable), ISP (minimal interfaces), DIP (constructor injection of abstractions).
